data.title: Commerzbank is ready for takeover talks with UniCredit - report
data.description:
Commerzbank (CRZBF) (CRZBY) Chairman Jens Weidmann invited UniCredit (UNCRY) (UNCFF) to start talks on the terms of a merger, dropping its prior resistance to an unsolicited takeover by the Italian lender, according to a media report on Friday.

The German lender's campaign to fend off an acquisition by UniCredit ended as the Italian bank built up a 48% stake in Commerzbank, which gives UniCredit the ability to determine shareholder resolutions.

"Even though we would have preferred things to be different, the balance ​of power at the next annual general meeting is clear," Wiedmann said, according to reporting by Reuters. He's calling for "constructive talks to establish the key parameters for staff, shareholders, and the customers of the bank."

The discussions are also in UniCredit's interest, he added. "There will be no shortcut via Berlin," Weidmann added.

Commerzbank AG (CRZBF) shares rose 1.1% in afternoon trading in Germany, while UniCredit (UNCRY) shares gained 1.1% in Italy.

data.title: XLRE reaches 52-week high after upbeat housing data; here are its top holdings
data.description:
The Real Estate Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLRE) rose 2.5% to a 52-week high of $46.08 on Friday after U.S. single-family new home sales in June exceeded expectations.

Data from the U.S. Census Bureau showed sales of new single-family homes rose 1.6% month over month to a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 628,000 in June from a revised 618,000 in May. The reading topped economists' expectations of 613,000, though sales remained 5.6% below year-ago levels.

A separate report underscored ongoing headwinds for the housing market. Mortgage rates climbed to their highest level in several months, weighing on mortgage demand while contributing to an increase in housing inventory.

The Real Estate Select Sector SPDR Fund tracks U.S. real estate companies, including REITs and real estate management and development firms. Housing market activity and financing conditions are closely watched indicators for the sector.

Top XLRE holdings by Momentum Grade:

  • Welltower (WELL) – Momentum Grade: A
  • Ventas (VTR) – Momentum Grade: A
  • Simon Property Group (SPG) – Momentum Grade: A-
  • Equinix (EQIX) – Momentum Grade: B
  • Prologis (PLD) – Momentum Grade: B-
  • Realty Income (O) – Momentum Grade: C
  • Public Storage (PSA) – Momentum Grade: C
  • Digital Realty Trust (DLR) – Momentum Grade: C-
  • American Tower (AMT) – Momentum Grade: D
  • CBRE Group (CBRE) – Momentum Grade: D

data.title: Midday Need to Know: Countries react to tariffs, Verizon hikes guidance & more
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages were mixed on Friday as oil prices decreased and U.S. stocks aimed to go higher. The indexes are on track for another losing week.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was unchanged, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -0.6%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was +0.2%.

Now, here are four news stories that broke overnight to watch out for:

U.S. imposes new tariffs on 60 trading partners under Section 301: The Trump administration early Friday enacted duties of 10% to 12.5% on imports from 60 countries, including Canada, Mexico, Japan, and the European Union, citing failures to enforce bans on goods made with forced labor. U.S. The new levies follow the Supreme Court's February ruling that struck down the original "Liberation Day" tariffs under IEEPA, forcing the administration to pivot to Section 301 of the Trade Act of 1974. Trading partners including Australia, Brazil, Canada, the EU, and Norway  pushed back on the justification, with EU Foreign Affairs Chief Kaja Kallas calling the tariffs "a negative surprise" and noting that European labor standards exceed those in the U.S.

Verizon posts mixed Q2 results but hikes guidance: Verizon Communications (VZ) reported adjusted earnings of $1.30 per share, beating the $1.27 consensus, while revenue slipped 0.7% year over year to $34.3 billion, missing estimates of $35.1 billion. The telecom giant posted its best consumer second-quarter postpaid phone net additions in five years at 184,000, while broadband net additions totaled 348,000. The company raised full-year guidance for the second consecutive quarter and increased its share repurchase target to up to $4.5 billion. Shares were down about 0.49% in premarket trading.

U.S. new home sales beat estimates in June: Single-family new home sales  rose 1.6% month over month to a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 628,000 in June, topping the 613,000 consensus estimate, according to Census Bureau data released Friday. The figure still represents a 5.6% decline from year-ago levels. The median sales price fell to $398,300, down 3.3% from May and 2.7% below June 2025. Housing supply stood at 9.3 months at the current sales pace, slightly lower than May's 9.4 months but up from 9.0 months a year earlier.

Paramount Skydance and Warner Bros. Discovery deal delay extended: A federal judge extended the closing  delay on Paramount Skydance's (PSKY) planned $110 billion acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) until at least August 17, according to a court filing Thursday. State attorneys general are seeking to block the mega media deal, with an August 3 hearing on a preliminary injunction still scheduled. The parties indicated they are having "productive discussions" regarding the injunction and are expected to file a joint update by noon Pacific time Friday.

data.title: Intel’s 15% earnings pop evaporates, erasing billions in market cap overnight
data.description:
Intel (INTC) shares reversed course sharply on Friday, erasing the stock's post market earnings rally and wiping out roughly $90 billion in market capitalization since Thursday afternoon.

The chipmaker’s stock had surged more than 15% following its latest earnings report, closing Thursday near $100.24 before jumping to $113.72 in after-hours trading. By Friday’s session, however, the shares were trading lower by about -4%, last seen near $96.40. 

The rapid pullback underscores how quickly investor sentiment can shift even after a strong earnings reaction. Despite the sharp reversal, Intel remains one of the market’s standout performers on a longer-term basis. The stock is still higher by +156.3% year-to-date, though it has declined -27.2% over the past month. 

Traders are closely watching whether the post-earnings enthusiasm can reassert itself or whether the recent volatility signals a more cautious stance toward the semiconductor giant in the near term.

ETFs with large exposure to INTC: (LINT), (FTXL), (FEPI), (XNTK), (KQQQ), (QQQA), (CARZ), (ADPV), (GOP), and (SOXX).

data.title: Gentex falls after Q2 revenue miss amid auto sales weakness
data.description:
Gentex (GNTX) shares fell 6% on Friday after the automotive supplier reported second-quarter earnings that topped expectations but posted revenue below estimates as weaker demand in several international markets weighed on sales.

The maker of auto-dimming mirrors reported adjusted earnings of 58 cents per share, beating analysts' estimates by 8 cents. Revenue fell short of expectations, coming in at $651.3M, about $18.3M below consensus.

Automotive revenue declined about 3% from the prior quarter, reflecting lower sales in Europe, Japan, South Korea, and China, partly offset by continued strength in North America.

The company left its 2026 revenue forecast unchanged at $2.65B to $2.75B, broadly in line with analysts' expectations of $2.69B. It, however, raised its gross margin outlook to 34.5%-35.5% from 34%-35%, lowered its operating expense forecast to $405M-$415M from $410M-$420M, and reduced planned capital expenditures to $115M-$125M from $125M-$140M.

Gentex also reaffirmed its 2027 revenue outlook of $2.80B to $2.90B.

The company faces a softer global vehicle production environment. The industry expects lower light vehicle production this year; persistent inflation, elevated interest rates, tariffs, high oil prices, and rising auto loan delinquencies continue to pressure vehicle demand.

Gentex, which holds an estimated 79% global share of the auto-dimming rearview mirror market, remains the dominant supplier in its niche, but analysts have warned that broader automotive industry headwinds could weigh on near-term growth despite its strong market position.

data.title: Nvidia CEO Huang joins X, signs letter to Congress on open AI models
data.description:
Nvidia (NVDA) CEO Jensen Huang has joined the X (formerly Twitter) social network, the tech titan said on Friday.

In his first post, he revealed that he signed a letter to the U.S. Congress on open AI models and the need for American leadership in the space.

“AI will transform every industry, power every company, and be built by every country,” Huang wrote in his post. “Open models strengthen safety and cybersecurity, accelerate innovation and diffusion, and enable sovereignty. The world needs both frontier closed models and frontier open models.”

The three-page letter was signed by Nvidia, along with a host of other tech industry heavyweights, including Meta Platforms (META), Box (BOX), CrowdStrike (CRWD), Microsoft (MSFT), and others. Twenty-five entities in total signed the letter.

Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella also posted on X about the letter and said they are “essential to a healthy AI ecosystem,” while adding they will “strengthen American competitiveness,”  expand economic opportunity, and protect national security.

“Our AI leadership will be judged not by one frontier AI model, but by whether the United States builds a strong, open ecosystem that diffuses into every sector,” the letter reads. “This is essential for creating opportunities for innovation and prosperity across the country. It requires expanding access to AI, encouraging competition, robust application layers, and giving Americans greater control over the technology they rely on. Open-weight models—AI models that anyone can download, inspect, modify, and run on their own infrastructure—are an important part of that foundation because they make advanced AI more accessible, adaptable, and widely available.”

The letter comes as the Trump administration has discussed putting on open source models. Earlier this week, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said the administration would sanction open-weight models from China if it found evidence of distillation, or essentially using proprietary information from American models, is verified.

On Wednesday, Michael Kratsios, the science advisor to President Trump, accused China-based startup Moonshot AI of “industrial distillation,” using Anthropic's (ANTHRO) Fable model to create its latest mode, Kimi K3.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

(This story has been updated with additional information.)

data.title: Noodles & Company raises fiscal 2026 adjusted EBITDA outlook to $34M-$38M, backed by portfolio optimization
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Noodles & Company (NDLS) Q2 2026

Management View

  • "This quarter marks one of the strongest performances since Noodles became a publicly traded company" (President, CEO & Director Joseph Christina). He added that "these results showcase that progress is happening faster than even we anticipated" and said they "reinforce our confidence in the long-term sustainability of the improvements we are making in the business" (President, CEO & Director Christina).
  • "The strong sales performance has continued in the third quarter, with quarter-to-date company-owned comparable sales up approximately 10%" (President, CEO & Director Christina). He also tied recent profit improvement to "the success of our portfolio optimization plan" and said the company is closing restaurants that are "in proximity to higher performing nearby restaurants" (President, CEO & Director Christina).
  • "In the second quarter, our total revenue was $127 million" and "system-wide comp restaurant sales during the second quarter increased 10.3%" (Chief Financial Officer Michael Hynes). He also highlighted that "our adjusted EBITDA in the second quarter increased 79% to $10.8 million" (Chief Financial Officer Hynes).

Outlook

  • "We have raised our fiscal 2026 guidance for revenue, margins, and adjusted EBITDA" (President, CEO & Director Christina).
  • "We're raising our full year 2026 guidance to the following: Total revenue of $485 million to $500 million, including comp restaurant sales growth of 8% to 11%" (Chief Financial Officer Hynes). He guided to "restaurant contribution margin between 16% and 17%" and "adjusted EBITDA between $34 million to $38 million" (Chief Financial Officer Hynes).
  • Compared with last quarter’s raised outlook, management lifted the revenue range (from $483 million-$498 million to $485 million-$500 million) and adjusted EBITDA range (from $32.5 million-$37.5 million to $34 million-$38 million), while maintaining expected company-owned closures at "30 to 35" and franchise closures at "5" (Chief Financial Officer Hynes; prior-quarter guidance from Chief Financial Officer Hynes).

Financial Results

  • "Net loss for the second quarter was $4 million, or a loss of $0.67 per diluted share" (Chief Financial Officer Hynes). He said the quarter "included a $4.8 million non-cash impairment charge, primarily related to our decision to close certain restaurants" (Chief Financial Officer Hynes).
  • "Company comp traffic during the second quarter increased 7.6%, and average check increased 3.8%, inclusive of 2.1% effective price during the quarter" (Chief Financial Officer Hynes). He also reported "company Average Unit Volumes in the second quarter increased 15.9% to $1.57 million" (Chief Financial Officer Hynes).
  • "Our restaurant contribution margin in the second quarter increased 440 basis points to 17.2%" (Chief Financial Officer Hynes). He attributed cost structure improvement to items including "cost of sales inflation in the second quarter was 0.7%" and "hourly wage inflation in the second quarter was 1.6%" (Chief Financial Officer Hynes).
  • "At the end of the second quarter, we had $1.3 million of available cash and our debt balance was $105.4 million" (Chief Financial Officer Hynes). He added the company "closed 2 company-owned restaurants and 2 franchise restaurants" in Q2 (Chief Financial Officer Hynes).

Q&A

  • Todd Brooks, The Benchmark Company, LLC, Research Division: asked how broadly ramen can expand and whether it becomes permanent; President, CEO & Director Christina said "we have now a consistent testing process out in our restaurants and we've got an 18-month calendar" and that for Q4 "we're finishing up the testing of multiple ramens" and "we haven't determined whether it's an LTO or a permanent item yet" (President, CEO & Director Christina).
  • Todd Brooks, The Benchmark Company, LLC, Research Division: asked what innovation is being lapped in 2H and how strong upcoming launches look; President, CEO & Director Christina said "we're lapping against the chili garlic ramen launch from last year at the end of September" and "we're really confident in the items that we've been testing" (President, CEO & Director Christina).
  • Todd Brooks, The Benchmark Company, LLC, Research Division: asked about loyalty scale and Boost Weeks cadence; Chief Financial Officer Hynes responded "our rewards program is about 25% of our sales currently" and said "for the full year '26, more of those" Boost Weeks are planned after leaning into them in the back half of last year (Chief Financial Officer Hynes).
  • Todd Brooks, The Benchmark Company, LLC, Research Division: asked why franchise comp lagged company comp; Chief Financial Officer Hynes said "we see a lot of variability in the franchise group and their performance" and added "we see overall health across the system" (Chief Financial Officer Hynes).

Sentiment Analysis

  • The analyst tone was positive and largely confirmatory, including "Congrats on the continuing improvement" and "It's very impressive" while focusing questions on menu innovation durability, loyalty/Boost Weeks, and franchise variability (Todd Brooks, The Benchmark Company, LLC, Research Division).
  • Management’s tone was positive and confident in prepared remarks and consistent in Q&A, including "we're really confident" and "we feel really strong and good about what's coming in the back half of the year" (President, CEO & Director Christina), alongside "we're raising our full year 2026 guidance" (Chief Financial Officer Hynes).
  • Versus last quarter, the tone stayed upbeat but became more emphatic on momentum and margin structure, with Q2 framed as "one of the strongest performances" and accompanied by another guidance raise (President, CEO & Director Christina; Chief Financial Officer Hynes).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance moved higher again, with full-year revenue and adjusted EBITDA ranges raised from Q1’s updated ranges to Q2’s updated ranges, while closures expectations stayed the same (Chief Financial Officer Hynes; prior-quarter guidance from Chief Financial Officer Hynes).
  • Strategy emphasis remained centered on operational execution, menu innovation, marketing discipline, and footprint optimization, but Q2 added more specificity on marketing efficiency and digital performance, including "second quarter digital channel comparable sales increased 18%" and impressions "nearly doubled" on "approximately 6%" higher spend (President, CEO & Director Christina).
  • Analyst focus remained similar (innovation pipeline, promotions/loyalty, sales-transfer effects), with Q2 adding emphasis on company vs. franchise comp dispersion (Todd Brooks, The Benchmark Company, LLC, Research Division).

Risks and Concerns

  • Portfolio optimization remained a material operational lever and source of one-time charges, with Q2 including "a $4.8 million non-cash impairment charge" tied to closing restaurants (Chief Financial Officer Hynes). Management also noted sales-transfer effects, estimating closures "benefited our second quarter comp restaurant sales by approximately 250 to 300 basis points" (Chief Financial Officer Hynes).
  • Capital structure and refinancing timing were flagged in the context of strategic review, as management said, "in connection with our ongoing review of strategic alternatives, we are continuing to review options with respect to the maturity of our credit facility in the third quarter of fiscal year 2027" (Chief Financial Officer Hynes).

Final Takeaway

Management characterized Q2 as a milestone quarter, citing accelerating comps, expanded restaurant contribution margin, and higher adjusted EBITDA, alongside a raised fiscal 2026 outlook. Leadership emphasized that results are being driven by tighter execution in restaurants, a more repeatable innovation calendar (including a Q4 ramen expansion), more disciplined marketing that leans on digital and loyalty, and continued footprint optimization with ongoing sales transfer to nearby stores—while also highlighting debt reduction plans and continued work on credit facility maturity options.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: War raises risks for debt-laden economies as bond yields climb, Brooks says
data.description:
Escalating geopolitical tensions are increasing risks for heavily indebted economies, with higher oil prices and rising government bond yields threatening to further erode already limited fiscal space, according to Robin Brooks.

In a blog, Brooks said history shows countries that enter prolonged conflicts with strained public finances often face mounting borrowing costs, weaker tax revenues and pressure to finance spending through debt issuance.

He argued that war comes at a time when fiscal positions across much of the G10 are already stretched.

Brooks said the latest conflict, while centered on the United States and Iran, is creating broader economic spillovers by driving oil prices higher, weighing particularly on Japan and highly indebted European economies.

He added that the situation comes as several central banks are already acting to limit bond yield increases to prevent debt crises.

Brooks said rising long-term government bond yields across the G10 appear to be driven primarily by expectations that central banks will tighten monetary policy in response to higher energy prices rather than by a sharp increase in fiscal risk premiums. He cautioned, however, that higher policy rates still worsen debt sustainability for heavily indebted governments.

Charts accompanying the analysis showed 2-year, 10-year and long-dated forward government bond yields for the United States, Germany, Japan, the United Kingdom, Italy and France. Brooks noted that the recent rise in 2-year yields (US2Y) has outpaced increases in longer-dated yields, suggesting markets are primarily pricing higher policy rates rather than a sustained deterioration in fiscal conditions.

He warned that the relatively muted rise in long-term yields could leave markets vulnerable if the conflict becomes prolonged. According to Brooks, markets appear to be pricing a relatively quick end to the conflict despite what he described as signs it is becoming a "growing quagmire."

Even under what Brooks described as the more benign interpretation, 10-year, 10-year forward yields in the United States, the United Kingdom and France have climbed to their highest levels in about two decades. "This is no time to be carrying around a big debt overhang," he wrote.

Here is the chart:
